content:
A story about frustration, growth, and the lessons every developer eventually learns.

14 min read
4 hours ago

--

At 2:13 AM, Alex stared at his monitor with tired eyes.
The room was silent except for the soft humming of his laptop fan. Empty coffee cups sat beside the keyboard like trophies from a battle he was clearly losing.
On the screen, one stubborn line of red text refused to disappear.
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined
Alex sighed.
He had copied the code from a YouTube tutorial.
Exactly.
Every single line.
Or at least… he thought he had.
He refreshed the page again.
Nothing.
Another error.
He searched Google.
Read five Stack Overflow answers.
Asked ChatGPT.
Watched another tutorial.
Still broken.
Finally, he leaned back in his chair and whispered the sentence thousands of beginner programmers have said before him:
“Maybe I’m just not smart enough for programming.”

The strange thing is…
Almost every professional software engineer has had this exact moment.
The difference between beginners who quit and developers who build incredible careers isn’t intelligence.
It isn’t mathematical talent.
It isn’t even writing perfect code.
It’s learning to avoid a handful of mistakes that quietly destroy progress before real learning even begins.
This isn’t another article about syntax.
This is about mindset.
Because programming is less about typing code…
…and far more about learning how to think.
Alex didn’t know it yet, but over the next year he would discover ten mistakes that almost every beginner makes.
The first one appeared before he even wrote his first real project.

Mistake #1 — Copying Code Without Understanding It
Alex’s first instinct was simple.
Whenever he got stuck…
He copied code.
From YouTube.
From GitHub.
From blogs.
From Stack Overflow.
If something worked…
Great.
If it didn’t…
He searched for another piece of code to copy.
At first, it felt productive.
His projects looked impressive.
Buttons worked.
Pages loaded.
Animations appeared.
Friends were amazed.
But then something unexpected happened.
One day, a tiny bug appeared.
Just one line.
Nothing major.
Except Alex had absolutely no idea where that line came from.
Or why it existed.
He realized something uncomfortable.
He had built a house without knowing how any of its walls were standing.

Many beginners confuse finishing a project with understanding a project.
They’re completely different.
Copying code is like memorizing the answers for a math exam.
You may pass today.
Tomorrow, when the questions change…
You’re lost.
Professional developers copy code too.
All the time.
The difference?
They stop after every important line and ask themselves questions.

• Why does this work?

• What happens if I remove this?

• Could I write it differently?

• What problem is this actually solving?
Curiosity turns copied code into knowledge.
Blind copying turns tutorials into a prison.

Alex’s Turning Point
Instead of copying entire files…
Alex started copying only five lines at a time.
Then he rewrote them from memory.
Then he broke them on purpose.
Changed variable names.
Deleted functions.
Added random values.
Something interesting happened.
For the first time…
The code stopped looking like magic.
It became a language.

Mistake #2 — Tutorial Addiction
After six months…
Alex had completed:

• 42 YouTube tutorials

• 18 Udemy lessons

• 11 React crash courses
His bookmarks folder was overflowing.
His watch history was endless.
His confidence…
Was almost zero.
Because every tutorial ended the same way.
The instructor built the project.
Alex followed along.
Everything worked.
Video finished.
Alex closed the browser.
And suddenly…
He couldn’t build anything alone.
This is called Tutorial Hell.
It’s one of the most common traps in programming.
Watching someone solve problems is not the same as solving them yourself.
Imagine watching someone ride a bicycle for 100 hours.
Would you suddenly know how to balance?
Of course not.
Programming works the same way.
Knowledge enters through watching.
Skill only grows through struggling.

The 70–30 Rule
Alex’s mentor gave him one piece of advice that changed everything.
“Spend 30% of your time learning.
Spend 70% building.”
Not copying.
Not watching.
Building.
Even ugly projects.
Even broken projects.
Especially broken projects.
Because bugs are teachers.
Tutorials are only maps.
The journey still belongs to you.

That weekend, Alex closed YouTube.
He opened an empty code editor.
No guide.
No instructor.
No tutorial.
Just one challenge.
“Build a simple To-Do app.”
It took him three days.
The tutorial version had taken only forty minutes.
But those three days taught him more than three months of watching videos.
And without realizing it…
He had finally started becoming a programmer.
When Learning Becomes Overwhelming
Alex thought the hardest part of programming was writing code.
He was wrong.
The hardest part was deciding what not to learn.
After finally escaping tutorial hell, excitement took over.
Everywhere he looked, someone was recommending a different technology.
One developer on YouTube insisted that React was essential.
Another claimed Vue was cleaner.
Someone on Reddit said Svelte was the future.
A blog argued that Rust would replace everything.
A LinkedIn post declared AI would write all code anyway.
Alex had a simple thought.
“Maybe I should learn all of them.”
That decision nearly ended his programming journey.

Mistake #3 — Learning Too Many Technologies at Once
His desktop soon looked like a digital museum.
Folders named:

• Python

• JavaScript

• React

• Vue

• Angular

• Node.js

• Docker

• Kubernetes

• Flutter

• Rust

• Go
Each folder contained half-finished projects.
Each course was only 30% complete.
Each notebook was filled with unfinished ideas.
Alex wasn’t learning.
He was collecting.
Collecting courses.
Collecting certificates.
Collecting bookmarks.
Collecting the illusion of progress.

One evening, his friend Maya visited.
She glanced at his monitor and laughed.
“How many programming languages do you actually know?”
Alex proudly answered,
“Well… I’m learning twelve.”
Maya smiled.
“No.”
She pointed at his screen.
“You’re learning none.”
The words stung.
But they were true.

Programming isn’t about how many technologies you’ve touched.
It’s about how deeply you’ve understood one.
Imagine trying to learn piano…
while also learning guitar…
violin…
drums…
and trumpet…
all in the same month.
You wouldn’t become a musician.
You’d become overwhelmed.
Programming is no different.
Depth beats breadth in the beginning.
Every single time.

Alex Changed His Strategy
Instead of chasing every new trend…
He chose one path.
JavaScript.
React.
Node.js.
Nothing else.
Whenever another exciting technology appeared…
He wrote it on a list called:
“Learn Later.”
That tiny notebook became one of the smartest decisions he ever made.
Because focus creates momentum.
Distraction creates frustration.

Mistake #4 — Avoiding Debugging
For months, Alex hated error messages.
Whenever one appeared…
His heart sank.
His first instinct was always the same.
Search Google.
Copy the solution.
Move on.
He treated debugging like punishment.
Professional developers see it differently.
They treat debugging like detective work.
Every bug leaves clues.
Every error message tells a story.
Every broken application is trying to explain what went wrong.
The question isn’t:
“Why is my code broken?”
The better question is:
“What is this bug trying to teach me?”

One rainy afternoon, Alex spent six hours trying to fix a login page.
He was convinced the API was broken.
He blamed React.
He blamed JavaScript.
He even blamed his internet connection.
Finally…
He noticed something.
A variable named:
userEmailwas being called as:
useremailOne lowercase letter.
Six hours.
One character.
He laughed so hard he almost cried.
That day he realized something important.
Computers are incredibly fast.
They are also incredibly literal.
They don’t understand what you meant.
Only what you wrote.

From that moment on, Alex stopped fearing bugs.
He became curious about them.
Instead of immediately searching for answers…
He began asking questions.

• What changed?

• When did it stop working?

• What does the error actually say?

• Can I reproduce the problem?
Slowly…
Debugging stopped feeling like failure.
It became a skill.
One that every great developer shares.

Mistake #5 — Believing Failure Means You’re Bad at Programming
Three months later…
Alex decided to apply for his first junior developer job.
He spent days polishing his portfolio.
He practiced coding interviews.
He imagined receiving an offer.
Instead…
He received an email.
“Thank you for your interest, but we’ve decided to move forward with other candidates.”
His stomach dropped.
Then another rejection came.
And another.
Five interviews.
Five rejections.
That evening, Alex opened his laptop.
He hovered over the uninstall button.
Maybe programming wasn’t for him after all.
Then his phone buzzed.
It was Maya.
She sent only one sentence.
“You’re confusing rejection with direction.”
Alex read it again.
And again.
Direction.
Not failure.
Every rejection showed him something to improve.
Algorithms.
Communication.
Git.
Testing.
Problem solving.
Each interview wasn’t closing a door.
It was pointing toward the next one.

Months later…
Alex finally received the email he had been waiting for.
“Congratulations. We’d like to offer you the position of Junior Front-End Developer.”
He smiled.
Not because he had become perfect.
But because he had become persistent.

A Lesson Worth Remembering
Programming is one of the few professions where feeling confused is part of the job description.
If you’re never confused…
You’re probably not learning anything new.
Every experienced developer still encounters bugs they don’t understand.
Still reads documentation.
Still searches online.
Still asks questions.
The difference is that they’ve stopped treating confusion as proof of incompetence.
They see it as evidence of growth.
Alex was beginning to understand that.
But his biggest lessons were still ahead.
Because the next mistakes were even more subtle…
Trying to memorize everything.
Writing unreadable code.
Ignoring soft skills.
And comparing yourself to developers with ten years of experience.
Those mistakes nearly cost him his dream career.
The Mistakes Nobody Warns You About
The morning Alex received his first job offer, he expected something magical to happen.
He imagined that once he became a professional developer…
The confusion would disappear.
The bugs would become easier.
The self-doubt would finally go away.
Instead…
On his very first day, a senior developer assigned him a task.
“Fix this small bug.”
“Small.”
It looked harmless.
Just one issue.
Alex smiled confidently.
Three hours later…
He was staring at over 8,000 lines of code written by people he had never met.
Nothing made sense.
Functions called other functions.
Variables had names like temp2FinalNew.
Files imported twenty other files.
He whispered to himself,
“I don’t belong here.”
Just then, David — the senior engineer — walked by.
He looked at Alex’s screen, smiled, and said something unexpected.
“Congratulations.”
Alex frowned.
“For what?”
David laughed.
“You’re confused. That means you’re finally working on real software.”
That single sentence stayed with Alex for years.
Because it revealed a truth nobody had ever told him.
Professional programmers aren’t people who know everything.
They’re people who know how to keep learning.

Mistake #6 — Trying to Memorize Everything
Alex carried a notebook everywhere.
Every JavaScript method.
Every React Hook.
Every CSS property.
Every Git command.
Every API syntax.
He believed great developers had incredible memories.
One afternoon, he watched David — the most experienced engineer on the team — open Google.
Again.
And again.
Documentation.
MDN.
Stack Overflow.
Official React Docs.
Alex couldn’t believe it.
“You still Google things?”
David laughed.
“Every single day.”
Then he
